evals.prompt: Are my cohorts getting worse over time? Honestly I can't tell.
evals.expected_output: Walks through the early-warning indicators framework since recent cohorts have insufficient history for direct long-window comparison. Should check the five indicators (day-30 drop, first-order AOV drop, channel mix shift, time-to-second-purchase, browse-to-buy ratio) and pull cohort survival to compare older cohorts where possible.
assertions: Applies the early-warning indicators framework
Calls modern.retention.cohort_survival to compare cohorts
Names the five indicators explicitly
Recommends a weekly dashboard for the five indicators
Notes that two or more indicators firing warrants investigation
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our blended LTV looks like it's improving but my gut says something is off.
evals.expected_output: Distinguishes channel-mix-driven LTV improvements from true product-engagement improvements. Should pull cohort LTV by channel to test whether the apparent improvement is real or is just spend shifting toward higher-retention channels. Should explain why blended LTV can rise without any underlying improvement.
assertions: Distinguishes channel-mix-driven LTV from product-engagement LTV
Calls modern.retention.cohort_ltv grouped by acquisition channel
Pulls revenue by channel to verify spend mix shift
Explains how blended LTV can rise without underlying change
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: What's the difference between my customer retention rate and my revenue retention rate?
evals.expected_output: Explains the two metrics and the four strategic combinations. High customer / low revenue → AOV problem. Low customer / high revenue → loyal core. Walks through which intervention applies to which combination. Should pull both metrics from the cohort data and identify which quadrant the brand is in.
assertions: Defines customer retention and revenue retention separately
Explains the four quadrants of high/low combinations
Pulls both metrics to identify the brand's quadrant
Names the appropriate intervention per quadrant
Recommends reporting both, not just one
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: What does it mean if my retention curve never flattens out?
evals.expected_output: Identifies a continuous-decline shape, which indicates a relationship-depth problem rather than a normal retention curve. Intervention target is product engagement and post-purchase activation, not retention messaging. Should distinguish from a healthy smile-shape curve.
assertions: Identifies continuous-decline shape from the description
Distinguishes from healthy smile-shape curves
Recommends product engagement intervention, not retention messaging
Calls modern.retention.cohort_survival to confirm the shape
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We acquired a bunch of customers via Meta last quarter. Are they retaining as well as our other customers?
evals.expected_output: Pulls cohort LTV grouped by acquisition channel. Should explain the typical channel retention hierarchy (referral > organic > paid search > paid social > programmatic) and note that Meta-acquired customers typically retain at lower rates than organic-acquired. Should walk through the implication for paid budget allocation.
assertions: Pulls cohort LTV by channel
Cites the typical channel retention hierarchy
Names Meta retention as typically lower than organic
Pulls first-touch attribution to clean channel labels
Connects findings to budget allocation question
References paid-ltv-optimization for the deeper paid analysis
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: I want to test a new welcome sequence and see if it improves retention. How do I set up the test?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the intervention test design framework. Pick the cohort (most recent full month with statistical power). Choose holdout (50/50 default). Primary metric: customer retention at month 3 or 6. Duration: minimum 90 days. Pre-register criterion for success and kill criterion. Should warn against testing on active customer file rather than a defined cohort.
assertions: Walks through cohort selection criteria
Recommends 50/50 holdout as default
Names primary metric as customer retention at month 3 or 6
Recommends minimum 90 day duration
Requires pre-registered success and kill criteria
Warns against active-file tests vs cohort tests
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: My second-purchase AOV is lower than my first-purchase AOV. Should I worry?
evals.expected_output: Possibly, depending on the trend. Walks through the AOV-trends-within-cohorts framework. Declining repeat AOV often indicates customers reverting to a single replenishment SKU after initial exploration — a depth-of-relationship problem that retention messaging will not solve. Recommends bundle and catalog work over flow tuning. References bundle-pricing for the structural fix.
assertions: Identifies declining repeat AOV as a depth-of-relationship signal
Recommends bundle and catalog work over flow tuning
References bundle-pricing for the structural fix
Pulls AOV by purchase number within cohort
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
